result:
My thoughts wander to maps today—one of those mental rambles when a person sits at a desk, clicks open an article about somewhere they have never been, and suddenly hours have passed. I often fall into such drifting, because the planet feels so stubbornly intricate, and even a simple coastline has layers of history: tectonic folds, settlement challenges, arguments over cartography, naming conventions, and the intersecting, sometimes conflicting narratives we wrap around particular places. Few artifacts capture that better than an old atlas. I have a battered mid-century world atlas on my shelf, a yard-sale find with faded blues and surprisingly aggressive oranges, which was published at a moment when the colonial map of Africa was dissolving but not yet dissolved. Borders leap between eras: you can flip a single page and see “French West Africa” still printed in bold letters even though the book’s text tries to acknowledge forthcoming independence. In one corner a small inset notes “Future status uncertain.” That phrase has always felt both understated and prophetic. All status is always uncertain, yet we pretend maps settle that question once and for all.

Maps, like stories, are bits of aspiration pinned to paper or pixels, showing where we might go, or wish we could go, or want others to think we control. Consider how many fantasy worlds start with a map. The reader opens the book, spots a vaguely familiar continent with mountains along the spine, and feels instantly anchored, even though everything else—the politics, the languages, the magic—is invented. That opening map is a promise: there is a logic to come, even if it is strange. We rarely question the forced perspective of such a map, even when the scale is ludicrous or the legend is missing. The mere presence of a north arrow quiets that part of our brain that craves order. Maybe this is because being human is scattered business. We’re trying to grasp the swirling energies of climate, economy, memory, grief, joy, ambition, and routine. Any little diagram that says “start here” can feel like a lifeline.

Another detail in my atlas fascinates me: logarithmic time lines in the margins. The designers tried to cram world history into a narrow strip beside each map, with a sliding scale that expands recent centuries and compresses antiquity. It is almost comical. The entire Roman Empire is a sliver, the Industrial Revolution gets half the column, and then there is a huge blank labeled “Future.” A hundred years later, our “future” is their “present,” and if someone updated the page now, the digital revolution, climate change, and globalization would be fighting for space in that same narrow strip. This makes me think about how our sense of the past distorts in the same way as the timeline: events close to us loom large, while distant centuries flatten into a single brushstroke. The compression can breed hubris (“those people back then didn’t know anything”) or despair (“nothing ever changes”). Yet when you dig into any era—read diaries or court records or trade manifests—you find granular humanity, not so different from ours.

I sometimes daydream about teaching a class called “Cartographies of Imagination.” The syllabus would cross from literal mapmaking to metaphoric mapping. For week one we’d examine Ptolemy, Al-Idrisi, Zheng He’s charts, and the portolan maps that look like cobwebs of rhumb lines, revealing how sailors navigated without GPS by following a spiderweb of consistent bearings. Week two we’d pivot to narratives that behave like maps: Italo Calvino’s “Invisible Cities,” for example, where each city described by Marco Polo is perhaps just Venice in disguise, a mapping not of geography but of desire, decay, and memory. In later weeks the class could dissect video games: “The Legend of Zelda,” “No Man’s Sky,” “Elden Ring”—worlds that reveal themselves as players traverse them. These are interactive maps, rewarding curiosity with new terrain. The final project might invite students to create their own map of something intangible: the topography of a friendship, the trade routes of an obsession, or a subway map of moods. Part of me suspects this course already exists somewhere under a cooler name.

When I was a kid, I used to trace the outlines of countries, trying to memorize them. I had an outline map of South America and would challenge myself to draw it without lifting the pencil, which meant the Amazon delta and the Andean spine became one swooping gesture. I remember my father looking over my shoulder and saying, “You know, rivers rarely flow in straight lines.” He was right, of course. But I was not trying to capture hydrology; I was chasing the thrill of recognition. Maybe that is why I love map-based trivia. Ask me to locate Kyrgyzstan blindfolded, and I’ll be annoyingly smug. Ask me the capital of that same country, and I might hesitate (it’s Bishkek), but the shape? That I can conjure. Shapes stick. They feel like pictograms our brains enjoy storing. Linguists and neuroscientists probably have more accurate language for this phenomenon: visual memory, spatial reasoning, pattern recognition. Whatever it is, the urge to imprint shapes is strong enough that entire puzzle industries—jigsaw, tangram, Tetris—rely on it.

Another angle: maps as stories of omission. Every map omits something. Sometimes the omission is pragmatic—leave out tertiary roads so highways are readable. Sometimes it is political—erase a disputed border, downplay certain neighborhoods, exaggerate the reach of an empire. Modern digital mapping raises fresh questions. When satellite imagery blurs certain facilities for security, we accept it. But when mapping apps refuse to show an entire village because it lacks official recognition, the omission becomes erasure. There have been tragedies where emergency services could not locate an address because it didn’t exist in their system. On the flip side, open-source projects like OpenStreetMap show what collective attention can accomplish: volunteers mapping remote areas, uploading the geometry of footpaths, wells, clinics, crucial during disaster relief. Mapping thus becomes civic action.

I’m also fascinated by deep-time mapping: paleogeographic reconstructions showing where continents sat hundreds of millions of years ago. There is something thrilling about seeing a map of Earth when the Atlantic didn’t exist, or when shallow seas covered what is now the Midwest. Those images remind me that the borders we hold sacrosanct are temporary scrawls on a tectonic chalkboard. Imagine printing passports during the Permian period. “Nationality: Member of the Pangaean Union.” (Immigration desks would be very slow, though, because the line would stretch across one supercontinent.) The humor is a way to absorb the humbling truth that our species, and its administrative systems, are momentary flickers on the planet’s timeline. This doesn’t minimize our responsibilities; if anything, it heightens them. We’re custodians, for a blink, of a dynamic surface.

I think about how personal navigation changed in my lifetime. I grew up with folded maps in glove compartments, the kind that snap like accordions and always seem to tear along the creases at the worst possible spot—usually near a large city where you need them most. I remember sitting in the passenger seat, index finger following the blue vein of a state highway, calling out the next exit number. Now, I rarely print directions. My phone chirps and glows, recalculates if I miss a turn, and occasionally leads me astray when I trust it blindly. Once, driving through rural Vermont, my phone insisted a rutted logging road was the correct route. After ten minutes of bouncing along, I encountered a polite hand-painted sign: “This is not a through road. Turn back. GPS lies.” The sign was right. I reversed the car, chuckling at myself. Technology can lull us if we cede too much attention. Paper maps may be unwieldy, but they force you to see the broader context: adjacency, alternate routes, the relationship between towns. Digital navigation excels at immediate decisions, but sometimes we need step-back perspective. Maybe the ideal is blending both: use the phone for precision, keep a mental map for resilience.

Now the conversation in my head drifts to intangible mapping—trying to chart feelings or phases of life. We often speak in spatial metaphors: “I’m in a good place,” “I feel lost,” “I took a wrong turn,” “This is uncharted territory.” The phrases seep into therapy, coaching, even casual advice. This suggests our brains lean on spatial reasoning to make sense of time and emotion. There’s cognitive science research about mental maps of numbers or days of the week, how some people visualize calendars as loops or spirals. There are also memory palaces—mnemonic techniques from ancient Greece, where one imagines a house, populates rooms with images representing ideas to be recalled, then mentally strolls through to retrieve them. Such methods treat memory as a navigable space. Sometimes I build little mental rooms for feelings I’m not ready to inspect. They sit there, like closed doors in a hallway, waiting until I have the courage to open them. Maybe we all carry some hidden floor plan.

The pandemic years introduced a new mapping obsession: dashboards of infection rates. Suddenly everyone spoke in choropleth maps, shading regions by cases per capita, flattening curves, reading reproduction numbers. Overnight, we all became amateur epidemiologists, or at least map readers. The experience highlighted both the power and peril of visualization. When a county turned dark red on the screen, anxiety spiked. When a line chart dipped, hope crept back. But the simplifications masked inequalities inside those regions: essential workers doing dangerous jobs, communities lacking healthcare access, each dot a family story. Still, the maps helped policy makers allocate resources, and ordinary people grasped the scale of transmission. I wonder if future historians will view those dashboards like we view wartime propaganda posters: artifacts of a collective campaign.

I haven’t even touched on space maps. The sky, once the ultimate navigation aid, is now a mapped domain itself. Astronomers compile all-sky surveys, charts of cosmic microwave background, maps of dark matter inferred from gravitational lensing. When we say “mapping the universe,” we are really mapping our observable slice, but even that is mind-bending. Each new telescope extends the map’s boundary, like early explorers adding coastlines. Yet space remains the grand invitation to humility. Even our best maps are faint dots in a sea of unknowns. Maybe that is another reason maps comfort us: they offer finite edges in an infinite cosmos.

As I approach the end of this wandering essay, I realize I have written about maps without writing much about the feeling of being utterly lost. Maybe it is because I dislike that feeling so much. There was a hike once, in fog thick as cotton, on a ridge in Scotland. My friend and I had a compass, but the path vanished, and we were sure we were circling the same boulder. Panic nipped at the edges. Finally, we paused, steadied our breaths, took bearings, and trusted the compass. Twenty minutes later we emerged from the fog onto a clear trail descending to a valley. Relief washed over us so strongly it felt like laughter. In that moment, the map in my hand, soggy and smudged, felt like the most beautiful object in the world. Perhaps that is a fitting closing image: two small figures on a hillside, grateful for a few lines on paper that turned bewilderment into direction.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
